title: Whats with Misato in Ep 1
wikitext:
[]: What's with Misato in Ep. 1?             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Thu Jun 18, 2015 9:53 am When the audience first meets Misato, we are almost immediately presented with NGE's signature "internal monologues" where we see the inner workings of the characters via their minds. However, the thoughts conveyed here are literally "very surface level." We see her mentally complaining about some of her external identifiers such as her dress and car and nothing of real substance. I think it's also interesting that the first person we see the inner thoughts of is female, given the series' emphasis on femininity(for better or worse regarding the circumstances of the characters and the whole sexuality vs. motherhood aspect). Furthermore, Misato's cheap way of thinking here is also rather jarring given the chronology of the series up to that point. If on were to have watched it chronological order we would have seen Misato a total of three times prior to this point: 1st during the 2nd Impact as she's being rescued by her dad/when she sees Adam spread its wings. 2nd when she is isolation and doesn't speak to anyone. 3rd time is when she goes to college and meets Ritsuko who remarks how talkative she is and comments on her sexual escapades with Kaji. Then, flash forward to the present of the show at the beginning of ep. 1 and our first glimpse of her thoughts aren't that deep. That's what I don't get...all the trauma/heartache she endured would suggest that she wouldn't be such a silly minded person... *On a related subject, watching the pre-Episode 1 chronology (with director's cut footage included) is kind of psychologically agonizing to watch as it starts out fairly sedate, gets very intense, then hopeful, then very bleak (especially when it comes to Asuka). Then it gets light hearted again, then bleak, then just weird and suddenly it's Rei II in Unit 00 for the first time. Anyone else think that starting with Fuyutski's origins as a professor is a rather odd place to start?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 18, 2015 2:20 pm If a narrative doesn't tell you things in chronological order there's probably a reason for it, you know? In this case it would kill the narrative tension if we just learned everything right away from Misato thinking about deep stuff... We're intended to see her as shallow and only later learn her hidden depths, just like everyone else in the show except Shinji and Gendo.

[]: Re: What's with Misato in Ep. 1?             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Jun 18, 2015 2:48 pm SawItAtAge10 wrote: Then, flash forward to the present of the show at the beginning of ep. 1 and our first glimpse of her thoughts aren't that deep. That's what I don't get...all the trauma/heartache she endured would suggest that she wouldn't be such a silly minded person...

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Jun 18, 2015 5:38 pm I see no reason why Misato can't be concerned about car payments. We can't be grim dark all the time.

[]:             Quote Post by Defectron » Thu Jun 18, 2015 6:44 pm I like to think about deep things but I also post stuff on this message board, people can do more then one thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Sat Jun 20, 2015 2:58 am First thought is that Noh structure starts with "draw in with familiar themes" and the boo hoo my clothes isn't something uncommon. Second thought which is probably more on the money is along the lines of what the people above me in this thread have said, which is that Misato isn't always thinking about the crappy things that she's been through. I scraped all the skin off the left side of my leg a few months ago (not comparable to second impact, but hang with me) and when it was all gross and healing, there were still times that despite how shitty it was, I still had it in me to think about lighter, more superfluous things. In short, people can be "surface level", as you put it, even when they've had nasty stuff happen to them.

[]: Re: What's with Misato in Ep. 1?             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Sun Jun 21, 2015 2:34 am SawItAtAge10 wrote: When the audience first meets Misato, we are almost immediately presented with NGE's signature "internal monologues" where we see the inner workings of the characters via their minds.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Jun 22, 2015 4:57 pm If it's worth anything at all, her dying words and final regrets in EoE included a bit about changing the carpet in her apartment. For as serious as she can be sometimes, she also equally light hearted and carefree, and that's part of what makes her such a wonderfully written character.

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 11:12 am Sachi wrote: If it's worth anything at all, her dying words and final regrets in EoE included a bit about changing the carpet in her apartment. For as serious as she can be sometimes, she also equally light hearted and carefree, and that's part of what makes her such a wonderfully written character.

[]:             Quote Post by Ari » Thu Jun 25, 2015 11:18 am Since we don't know anything about Misato at that point in the series, any "deeper" things said by her would probably be some form of foreshadowing. Foreshadowing to what we learn later in the series would ruin its impact, and it wouldn't fit with the nature of Misato's conflict: Misato in general is someone who lives a very "on the surface" life, and what we see of her in a lot of Evangelion is exactly that.

[]:             Quote Post by zlink64 » Sat Jun 27, 2015 10:12 am SawItAtAge10 wrote: If so, does one get the feeling that Anno was kind of trashing the concept of this type of over-emphasis on "familiarity/resemblance" in EoE by depicting it in the most horrific/strange/esoteric ways possible?

Furthermore, Misato's cheap way of thinking here is also rather jarring given the chronology of the series up to that point. That's what I don't get...all the trauma/heartache she endured would suggest that she wouldn't be such a silly minded person... *On a related subject, watching the pre-Episode 1 chronology (with director's cut footage included) is kind of psychologically agonizing to watch as it starts out fairly sedate, gets very intense, then hopeful, then very bleak (especially when it comes to Asuka). Anyone else think that starting with Fuyutski's origins as a professor is a rather odd place to start?
